coredata.dc:title: Pioneering molecularly-level Fe sites immobilized on graphene quantum dots as a key activity descriptor in achieving highly efficient oxygen evolution reaction

coredata.dc:description: The emergence of efficient electrocatalysts to facilitate the thermodynamically demanding and kinetically challenging oxygen evolution process (OER), which includes the coordinated transfer of four protons and four electrons, remains an issue. The effective transfer of energy encourages molecular-level control over key redox transitions involving small-molecule substrates (O2, and H2O), at or near electrode surfaces. While many molecular catalysts have been shown highly active for OER, immobilizing those onto the heterogenous solid matrices is challenging. Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a carbonaceous sub-class, was used as the solid matrices to conjugate the molecular catalyst onto its surface owing to its high surface area and electronic conductivity. Through the coordinated environment of the conjugated material, the molecular Fe sites on the GQDs surface exhibited outstanding OER features, achieving an ultra-low overpotential of 223 mV and 241 mV at a current density of 50 and 100 and mA/cm2, respectively, and excellent stability over 24 h. Our results marked a pioneering step in that through conjugation, these molecular sites were well dispersed at the GQDs matrices and were intrinsically active for OER.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Heterogenization of the molecular Fe2+. (a). Schematic illustration of molecular Fe immobilized onto GQDs, (b) TEM images of as-synthesized GQDs-Fe. Inset image: HR-TEM of GQDs-Fe, (c) FTIR spectra of p-GQDs, Fe(acac)2, and GQDs-Fe, (d) Raman spectra of GQDs and GQDs-Fe, (e) photoluminescence excitation-emission-intensity spectra of GQDs-Fe. Inset: GQDs-Fe solution under 365 nm UV light.

Molecular Fe2+ sites formation (a). The illustrated conjugation model of GQDs-Fe, (b). Normalized Fe K-edge XAS spectra of Fe foil, FeO, Fe(acac)2, Fe2O3, and GQDs-Fe, inset: the magnified pre-edge region, (c). Corresponding Fourier-transformed EXAFS spectra at Fe K-edge, (d-f). Wavelet Transform of the Fe K-edge. Fitting results of the EXAFS spectra of Fe2O3, Fe(acac)2, and GQDs-Fe.
Local configurations upon immobilization. The soft X-ray absorption near-edge structure (NEXAFS) spectra of (a). C K edge and (b). O K edge of p-GQDs and GQDs-Fe.

Electrocatalytic activity of GQDs-Fe (a). iR-compensated LSV curves of GQDs-Fe and p-GQDs in 1 M KOH (5 mV/s), (b). CV polarization curves of the GQDs-Fe and p-GQDs, (c). Corresponding Tafel slopes, and (d). Current density as scan rate function (measured at 0.2 V vs. Hg/HgO/1.0 M NaOH) to determine the double-layer capacitance (Cdl).

Catalytic optimization (a). iR-compensated LSV curves of GQDs-Fe (0.25 M), (0.1 M), and (0.01 M) in 1 M KOH (5 mV/s), (b). Corresponding Tafel slopes, and (c). XPS full resolution spectra of GQDs-Fe (0.25 M) and (0.1 M).

Active site investigation (a). The chelation process of the Fe2+ ions from GQDs in a sketch, (b). LSV polarization curves of the GQDs-Fe before and after chelation, (c). CV curves of the GQDs-Fe before and after chelation, and (d). UV–Visible spectra of GQDs-Fe reacted with EDTA solution and the EDTA solution only.

OH− ions adsorption at the molecular single Fe sites (a). Redox constant (ks ) of the catalytic sites in the GQDs, GQDs-Fe, GQDs-Co, and GQDs-Ni, (b). Pourbaix chart, a plot of redox potentials vs. various pH values of KOH solution.
Schematic illustration of immobilization of molecular Fe2+ ions for the metal active sites of the OER.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of efficient electrocatalysts to facilitate the thermodynamically demanding and kinetically challenging oxygen evolution process (OER), which includes the coordinated transfer of four protons and four electrons, remains an issue. The effective transfer of energy encourages molecular-level control over key redox transitions involving small-molecule substrates (O2, and H2O), at or near electrode surfaces. While many molecular catalysts have been shown highly active for OER, immobilizing those onto the heterogenous solid matrices is challenging. Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a carbonaceous sub-class, was used as the solid matrices to conjugate the molecular catalyst onto its surface owing to its high surface area and electronic conductivity. Through the coordinated environment of the conjugated material, the molecular Fe sites on the GQDs surface exhibited outstanding OER features, achieving an ultra-low overpotential of 223 mV and 241 mV at a current density of 50 and 100 and mA/cm2, respectively, and excellent stability over 24 h. Our results marked a pioneering step in that through conjugation, these molecular sites were well dispersed at the GQDs matrices and were intrinsically active for OER.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an alternative energy source to fossil fuel, sustainable energy conversion and storage technologies, such as fuel cell, metal-air batteries, and water electrolyzer has pinning extensive attention on mitigating cleaner way of H2 production/consumption [1]. The overall water splitting involves an oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode, a rate-determining step process, which exhibits substantial overpotential and energy loss due to the sluggish kinetic reaction that generates multiple electron–proton transfers [2–6]. The existing state-of-the-art OER electrocatalyst, the noble metal group (i.e. the precious metal oxides) promotes superior turnover frequencies and overpotential, yet the scarcity and cost ineffectiveness limit their broader implementation [7]. The development of the highly active, durable, and scalable electrocatalyst, therefore, is becoming important yet challenging.

Of the immense category of available OER electrocatalysts, homogenous molecular species, particularly, have drawn interest due to their molecularly well-defined active site that is fully exposed to the reactants, and allows catalytic activity tuning systematically, as well [8,9]. Particularly, molecular catalyst—molecular transitional metal and an active redox ligand, have shown selective and efficient for catalysis [10–12]. The downsized active sites, in contrast to the relatively bulk version, to the near-atomic scale that multiply the ratio of the surface-to-core, maximize the way to acquire high turnover frequency (TOF) during catalysis [13]. As a result, such inherited features make systemic customization of its catalytic selectivity and efficiency possible. Despite its largely unknown comprehensive studies, these molecularly precise active site enables new light in adopting molecular precision in the design and mechanistic study of the heterogeneous catalysts. The dependence of the molecular catalyst on their environment has made these catalysts perform on the homogenous solution to sustain the 100 % utilization of their active sites. Though, however, the recovery from these solutions might be difficult to preserve the whole structure [11]. Matters to the fact, the non-aqueous medium is the only option for numerous molecular catalysts to dissolve, remain stable, or be active [14]. While aqueous electrolytes are commonly utilized in a real-world applications, the challenge to assemble them into electrolysis devices draws it further from becoming practical.

Up until recently, there was a sizable concept library that might address these issues. One of the particular options is heterogenization, which involves immobilizing the molecular catalysts onto a heterogeneous solid matrix. In parallel with the efforts to heterogeneous immobilization, significant advances have been called out to confining molecular catalysts onto the solid matrix for a target reaction, through either covalent linkages [8,15], in which connecting within thin films of metal (covalent) frameworks, or non-covalent linkages [16,17]. These commonly obtained from the immobilization of transition metal (TM) onto solid matrix catalysts, had exhibited good competency in catalytic activity toward OER [18,19]. Theoretically, the catalytic kinetics was governed by the electronic and geometric configuration of the molecular catalysts, that in turn influenced the redox properties and chemisorption states of the intermediates. The immobilized molecular catalysts on the supports retain the well-defined structures which might be tuned by adjusting the ligand configuration, enabling the structural tuning at a molecular level [20]. Nowadays, an emerging carbonaceous materials class sheds light on the most prominent matrix for molecular catalyst loading owing to their strong electronic conductivity, enormous surface area, high loading capacity, and excellent stability [16,21]. Considering the ability to construct diverse covalent linkages at carbon atoms by surface functionalization, the latter strategy of carbon-based matrices is particularly appealing. A series of findings also suggest that electron transport is crucially determined by the engineering of the electrical wires configurations [22] or the distances shortening [14] across the surface matrix and the active sites, fine-tuning the overall reaction rates.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a revolutionary type of zero-dimensional nano-carbon featuring graphene segments in nanoscale size (5–20 nm), demonstrated considerable potential as an effective OER electrocatalyst. Offering many edge and defect sites, as is typical of GQDs, is advantageous for staging catalytic processes, which frequently occur on the edge than in the basal plane [23]. Engineered nanoarchitecture or molecular carbon, —for example surface functionalization, geometric structuring, and heteroatom doping, have been used to develop novel concepts [24–31]. The latest research by Xia et al. demonstrated graphene quantum dots matrix as a support for single-atom catalyst (SAC). It was reported that GQDs' ability to provide multiple anchoring sites enabled the formation of high loading of transition-metal atoms, which in turn vastly increased the performance in electrochemical CO2 reduction [32]. In parallel with OER electrocatalyst studies, the non-noble/transitional metal catalyst [33], particularly the Fe-based system [34] had shown remarkable activity towards OER in an alkaline medium, despite their counterparts and size [35–38]. Fe oxides/(oxy)hydroxides became the most representative system [39,40], where substantial evidence of the mechanistic insight suggests that the OER mechanism involves the conversion of Fe2+ to Fe3+ cations [41]. Not a single study, to the best of our knowledge, has established an in-depth, comprehensive understanding of the role of the metal-support interaction in the OER mechanism. Designing the state-of-art molecular catalyst with a competent immobilization strategy is required to cross over to heterogeneous catalysis.

Given the aforementioned discussions, we, herein pursued a novel catalytic immobilization strategy by grafting molecular Fe2+ ions onto the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) matrix that provided suitable anchoring spots. The schematic representation of the immobilization of the molecular Fe catalyst onto the GQDs (GQDs-Fe) was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Distinctive from the typical OER active site, our finding showed that a single Fe2+ ion might be capable of originating the OER active sites. In addition to their high stability over a 24-hour period, the proposed GQDs-Fe also demonstrated an ultralow overpotential of 223 mV at a current density of 50 mA/cm2.

Iron (II) acetylacetonate (CH3COCH = C(O)CH32Fe, ≥99.98 % purity) was procured from Sigma Aldrich. Similarly, Sigma-Aldrich provided the citric acid (C6H8O7, ACS reagent, ≥ 99.5 % purity) for this study. The potassium hydroxide (KOH) used in this research was sourced from Fisher Chemical. Deionized water (DIW) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ/cm at 25 °C was utilized throughout the experiment. All materials were used as supplied, without the need for additional purification, relying solely on their original quality and specifications.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was prepared via hydrothermal treatment just as the previous literature with minor modifications, where the citric acid aqueous solution (1 mg/mL, 40 mL) was moved in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. Following cooling down to room temperature, the light yellow GQDs solution was dialyzed against DI water for 20 min using a dialysis tube (Spectrum Lab. Inc, 3000 Da) to detach impurities. Identically, a typical synthesis of GQDs-Fe was conducted using the similar steps, where Fe(acac)2 with varied concentrations (0.25, 0.1, and 0.01 M) were added into the citric acid solution (1 mg/mL, 40 mL), then moved into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting GQDs and GQDs-Fe (light yellow solution) were dialyzed against DI water for 20 min using a dialysis tube and centrifugation to detach impurities and unreacted chemicals. The solution then stored in a storage temperature (−18 oC) prior to the usage. Meanwhile, for a comparison, the GQDs-Fex was prepared by dispersing the 0.1 M Fe(acac)2 in DMF and then heating it to 80 °C for 12 h (1 atm). The resultant solution was centrifuged for the supernatant removal, washed several times with DI water and ethanol, and dried in an oven (60 °C) overnight to obtain the powder. GQDs-Ni and GQDs-Co were prepared in a similar way, except the Ni and Co precursor, Ni(acac)2 and Co(acac)2, respectively.

The electrode for evaluating OER performance was Ni foam (FuCell Co., Ltd., Taoyuan, Taiwan). Ni foam (1 cm x 3 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned with 1.0 M HCl for 30 min before being washed with DI water for several times. The Ni foam were subsequently dried on a hotplate at 70 °C. Epoxy tapes (3 M Company, thickness 60 m) were used to mark the active electrode region of 1 cm x 1 cm of the Ni foam. The as-obtained catalysts (GQDs-Fe, GQDs-Ni, and GQDs-Co) were drop casted directly over the active region of the Ni foam (loading: 300 μL−1). In order to obtain the electrocatalysts ink of GQDs-Fex (1 mL), the electrocatalysts (10 mg) were dissolved into a mixture solution consist of DI water (200 μL), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 750 μL), and Nafion® 117 solution (50 μL) under ultrasonication to attain a homogenized ink. Then, the catalyst ink was subjected to the Ni foam by drop-casting method (loading: 3.0 mg/mL), followed by drying on the surface of hotplate (70 °C).

The field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-6500F, JEOL) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai 20 G2 S-Twin Transmission Electron Microscope) were employed for assessing their elaborate structures. To obtain the membrane IR spectra, we utilized an exact attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) apparatus from PerkinElmer, USA. For accurate Raman measurements at room temperature, we utilized a JASCO 5100 spectrometer with an excitation laser wavelength of 532 nm. Thin films of the materials were deposited by drop-casting onto Si substrates, followed by a 24-hour curing procedure at ambient temperature. In order to maintain precision, laser power was controlled at 0.1 mW to prevent laser heat generation, and Raman shifts were calibrated using the 520 cm−1 peak on a silicon wafer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected with a JASCO V676 absorbance spectrophotometer and a pair of 1 cm quartz cuvettes. To assure accuracy, the PL spectra were scaled proportionally to the measured excitation power. For in-depth analysis, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) were carried out at the Taiwan Photon Source (TPS) located at the BL24A1, BL17C1, and BL20A beamline stations, National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC), Hsinchu, Taiwan. These techniques allowed us to examine changes in electron states and gain detailed structural information about the as-prepared materials. We calibrated the binding energies using the C 1 s (284.6 eV) for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Finally, the X-ray diffraction pattern of the GQDs-Fex was identified using a state-of-the-art Bruker X-ray Diffractometer (D2 Phaser) with monochromatized Cu K radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å).

In our experimental setup, we employed a standard three-electrode arrangement submerged in oxygen-saturated 1.0 M KOH at ambient temperature in our experiment. An Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT302N with FRA32M, Metrohm Autolab) was used to deliver accurate control and monitoring. For the electrodes, we employed Ni foam substrates coated with the electroactive materials we designed. A platinum rod served as the counter electrode, while Hg/HgO (in 1 M NaOH) served as the reference electrode. We used the catalyst aqueous solution directly onto the Ni foam substrate. To evaluate the electrochemical performance, we conducted linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, corrected with iR compensation. After each step, we measured the ohmic drop through impedance spectroscopy and used it for iR compensation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed within a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz, with a 5 mV amplitude, revealing insights into the system's behavior. We employed cyclic voltammogram data from the non-faradaic zone (1.11 to 1.21 V vs. RHE) at varied scan speeds (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV s−1) to calculate the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA). To standardize all potentials, we transformed them into potential vs. the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using Eq.
(1) :

Here, EHg/HgO/1 M NaOH represents the converted potential vs. Hg/HgO, with E°Hg/HgO/1 M NaOH = 0.165 V at 25 °C. The experimentally measured potential vs. the RHE reference is denoted as ERHE, ensuring consistency and comparability of our results.

The turnover-frequency (TOF) was calculated using the following equation.
TOF = ( J × A ) / ( 4 × m × F )

The parameter
J
represents the current density (OER) obtained from LSV curves at low scan rate (5 mV/s) to avoid current influence from electrode capacitance,
A
denotes the electrode’s geometrical surface area,
F
signifies a Faradaic constant, and
m
is the total number of electroactive Fe sites, calculated from the integrated charge amount area of metal redox peak.

The Laviron method was assessed to determine the redox constants, according to the previous study [42]. To calculate the redox constants, cyclic voltammogram at varied scan speeds (10––100 mV s−1) were employed. The Laviron equation [43] is written as Eq.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular Fe2+ ions heterogenization on GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Similarly to pristine GQDs (p-GQDs), the GQDs-Fe was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal process. Meanwhile, the p-GQDs, as reported before, were synthesized using citric acid as the precursor (provided in Figure S1 ) [25]. The structure–activity relation was investigated by identifying the structure of the p-GQDs as the matrix of immobilization. The TEM image (Fig. S1
b) revealed the morphology of the p-GQDs, with a well-crystallized particle size of ∼ 6 nm and a distinct lattice constant of 0.214 nm, matching the graphene layer’s (100) direction hexagonal lattice spacing [44]. A significant quantum and edge effect of the GQDs enabled them to exhibit unique photoluminescence (PL) behavior, manifested as bright fluorescence. Fig. S1 c depicted the photoluminescence excitation-emission-intensity spectrum of the p-GQDs, which addressed a relatively strong excitation and emission wavelength at 340 nm and 430 nm, respectively. Most crucially, to pinpoint the role of the immobilization issues, the XPS was conducted, enabling the chemical state and composition profile of the p-GQDs. The high-resolution of C 1 s spectrum deconvolution (Fig. S1 d) noticed a variety of carbon configurations ranging from single to double-bonds, such as O − C = O (293.8 eV), C − O (288.3 eV), C = O (290.8 eV), and C = C (284.9 eV), confirming the presence of − O containing functional group. Specifically, those major functional groups in conjunction with the copious edge site of the GQDs could potentially serve as an ideal anchoring site for the molecular Fe2+ ion [32].

The molecular Fe was immobilized on the GQDs matrix by hydrothermal process, as illustrated in the Fig. 1 a. Theoretically, the Fe2+ ions were delivered by Fe(acac)2 and immobilized on the GQDs matrix through the − O functional groups under a moderate environment. A number of different characterizations were carried out to better understand the origins of the enhanced catalytic activity. The TEM image (Fig. 1 b) displayed an identical morphology of GQDs-Fe to p-GQDs, with no evidence of structural alterations. However, the particle size appeared slightly bigger, around ∼ 7 nm upon the introduction of molecular Fe2+ ions. Interestingly, the TEM picture (inset Fig. 1 b) revealed no crystal structure of Fe oxides, suggesting it might be dispersed on a molecular level. The SEM-EDX ( Figure S2 ) revealed the uniform distribution of the C, O, with particular signals indicative of Fe, suggesting the successful immobilization of Fe ions. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1 c) was employed to identify the immobilization of the Fe2+ ions within the GQDs. The p-GQDs consistently exhibited vibration spectra similar to previously reported GQDs, with two strong peaks at 1215 and 1690 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of C = O in the hydroxyl groups and C = C bonds, respectively, which are characteristic of graphene structures [45]. Comparable vibration spectra were also observed in GQDs-Fe. However, both of those vibration spectra appeared weaker which might due to the Fe2+ ions immobilization. Interestingly, a distinct vibration was identified in the FTIR spectra that are associated with the methylene group, a characteristic band of Fe(acac)2. The characteristic bands of the methylene group were recorded, and they exhibited a negative shift from 2990 cm−1 to 2983 cm−1 in GQDs-Fe2+ while being absent from GQDs, providing strong evidence that the Fe sites in GQDs-Fe2+ remain well-defined at the molecular level. Furthermore, the Fe ions did not break down into any Fe-oxides throughout the ligation process. The negative shift indicates a sudden loss of electron density from the methylene group after ligation, which may suggest the successful binding of Fe2+ to GQDs through the rearrangement of the electron distribution in the adjacent Fe2+ sites [42].

Assessing the structural alteration of the Fe2+ ions on the GQDs, Raman, and photoluminescence (PL) were conducted. As shown by Fig. 1 d, the Raman-featured defects-related D to G bands ratio (ID/IG) was measured, and the p-GQDs showed typical defective graphene fragments with a broader ID/IG ratio of 1.02, suggesting a disordered structure-rich of the p-GQDs [46]. Meanwhile, the GQDs-Fe displayed a drastic decline in ID/IG ratio to 0.78, convincing the fact that the molecular Fe2+ ions were most probably occupying the defective side of the GQDs. Furthermore, the excitation-emission-intensity photoluminescence spectra at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1 e) noticed a red-emissive GQDs-Fe of both excitation and emission wavelength as compared to those of p-GQDs, centered at 400 and 470 nm, respectively. This was attributed to the distorted charges due to the strong interaction between Fe2+ ions and the adjacent electron of the GQDs matrix that led to the increased separation extent of the positive and negative charges that gave both excitation and emission red-shift [47]. Additionally, the red shift, attributed to the edge localized exciton reflected the size-dependent of GQDs. The increasing size of GQDs-Fe2+ as displayed by TEM, led to the aromatic sp2 nano-domains overlap due to the re-distribution of the electron as a result of immobilization [48]. The above-mentioned results provided support for the defect-occupying molecular Fe2+ on the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation of the molecular Fe2+ sites
ce:section.ce:para:
The formation of the molecularly-level Fe grafted onto GQDs matrices (Fig. 2
a) was further studied by X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS). A look at the clear local coordination environment and the oxidation state was presented by the normalized Fe K-edge XANES feature (Fig. 2 b), which showed a nearly identical rising-edge energy location of GQDs-Fe2+ and its precursor, Fe(acac)2, yet it showed a clear distinction to that of FeO, Fe2O3, and Fe foil. Thus, it depicted minimal changes within Fe ions upon the ligation process. Additionally, it matched the peak of Fe(acac)2, indicating the valence state of Fe ions remained + 2. Pinpointing the change in the local geometry coordination, the expanded pre-edge feature was observed in the inset figure of the XANES spectra, whereas it slightly shifted toward a low-energy direction (∼0.3 eV) and appeared less intensified, suggesting minor changes in the local coordination geometry of the Fe sites. Prior studies had reported the so-called biased octahedral geometry of the Fe(acac)2 analyzed by the XRD, hence we assumed that the coordination geometry would remain as octahedral Fe sites [49]. Besides, it confirmed that the ligand domain at the two neighboring Ni sites was nearly identical. By the Fourier-transformed extended x-ray absorption fine structure (FT-EXAFS) as shown in Fig. 2 c, the first shell of the Fe center (belongs to Fe-O bond) depicted a trivial increase at a radial distance (R’) of 1.56 Å, that might be possible evidence of the Fe2+ and GQDs bonding, through its O-functional group [50]. The outer shell, assigned to Fe-Fe bonds (2.37 Å) however, showed drastically reduced intensity to that of the reference, pointing to the sole presence of the molecular Fe sites, in good agreement with the absence of the oxide’s crystal lattice of the TEM images. These confirmed the conjugation process of the Fe2+ ions to the GQDs.

To gain further insight into the molecular dispersion of Fe in GQDs-Fe, wavelet transforms (WT) of Fe K-edge EXAFS oscillations were utilized due to their powerful resolution in both k and R spaces. Intriguingly, both Fe(acac)2 (Fig. 2 e) and GQDs-Fe (Fig. 2 f) displayed identical features in the WT contour plots, with only one intensity maximum observed at ∼ 4.6 Å−1 corresponding to Fe-O coordination. This finding provided compelling evidence that the Fe atoms were dispersed molecularly within the GQDs matrix and that there were no changes in the configuration of Fe during the immobilization process, which is consistent with the previous FTIR result. Importantly, the maximum intensity features of GQDs-Fe in the WT contour plots were clearly distinguishable from those of Fe foil (Fig. S3
a), which represented the Fe-Fe contribution. The finding eliminated the possible existence of Fe clusters or nanoparticles in our catalyst. Furthermore, the maximum intensity features of GQDs-Fe were distinct from those of both FeO (Fig. S3 b) and Fe2O3 (Fig. 2 d), which represented the oxide forms. These results strongly suggest that the Fe sites in GQDs-Fe are identical to those in Fe(acac)2 rather than exist in an oxide form and that the Fe atoms are dispersed molecularly within the GQDs matrix, ruling out the formation of Fe-based clusters or oxides.

The remarkable insight of the bonding configuration following immobilization of the molecular Fe2+ ions was unveiled by the X-ray absorption near-edge structure (NEXAFS) spectra. The C bonding environment was depicted in Fig. 3 a, where the C K edge showed consistent features of the typical graphene material, including unoccupied π* (shaded green region) and excited σ* (shaded yellow region) bands, indicating the continuing graphitized carbon matrix upon immobilization. The absorption band around 284.0 eV was assigned to the C 1 s-π*C=C transition [51]. The major changes were observed in the GQDs-Fe, where it exhibited a minor shoulder peak at 287.1 eV and even a diminished peak at 288.8 eV, corresponding to π*C=O and π*−COOH structures, respectively [52]. Consistent with the EXAFS result, those narrowed intensities suggested the reduction of the O-functional groups of the p-GQDs after the molecular Fe2+ ions immobilization, which could be linked to the new binding between O-functional groups and Fe2+ ions. The noticeable presence of C = O and − COOH groups within GQDs could be inferred as potential sites for molecular Fe2+ ion attachment, indicating their role as defective sites. The observed intensity increase in −π*C=C band and concurrent decrease in σ*C−C band corresponded to the formation of sp2-coordinated carbon, confirming the altered local carbon configuration upon the immobilization. Meanwhile, the O K edge spectra (Fig. 3 b) provided further information about the conjugation of the Fe2+ ions within the GQDs. Here, the pre-edge feature exhibits an important major increase on peak shoulder at 528.5 eV of the GQDs-Fe, corresponding to the TM3d-O2p state that most likely arises from Fe2+-O 2p hybridization [53]. Hence, it confirmed the bonding of Fe2+ ions with somewhat O-functional groups of GQDs, enabling the Fe2+ ions immobilization, in line with the EXAFS result. The rest of the sharp peaks at 532.2 eV, 533.9 eV, and 538.3 eV corresponded to the O 1 s-π*C=O, π*C−O−C, and σ*C=O, respectively appeared less intensified on GQDs-Fe, which further confirming the carbon matrix distortion upon the Fe2+ ions immobilization.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to support the heterogenization of Fe2+ ions throughout GQDs. The XPS full scan spectra (Fig. S4
a) revealed the presence of elements such as C and O in both p-GQDs and GQDs-Fe, with the exception of Fe in the GQDs-Fe. Referring to the XPS atomic composition ( Table S1 , ESI), the C/O element ratios underwent a change following the immobilization of the Fe2+ ions, with the value declining from 0.77 to 0.50, suggesting substantial changes in the carbon matrix. Furthermore, the detailed analysis of the high-resolution XPS C 1 s spectra (Fig. S4 b) demonstrated major distortion in C-based functional groups after the immobilization, providing an additional notion of altered bonding as Fe2+ ions occupied defective sites in GQDs. This result was identical to the NEXAFS spectra, which verified the altered local configuration of the carbon matrix upon the immobilization. The existence of Fe species at GQDs-Fe was further verified by high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectra (Fig. S4 c), which revealed two primary peaks at 710.0 eV and 723.8 eV, corresponding to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 core levels [54]. The Fe 2p deconvolution spectra revealed a prominent peak in both core levels assigned to the Fe2+, in good agreement with the EXAFS result. Hence, based on our current understanding, the valence state of Fe remained + 2 subsequent to its immobilization onto the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic evaluation of the immobilized Fe2+ ions at GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic OER activity of GQDs-Fe was performed using the typical three-electrode system. The potential and current-dependent polarization curves were recorded after iR compensation. The apparent linear scanning voltammogram (LSV) curves at a low scan rate (5 mV/s), as shown in Fig. 4 a showed a significant increase in the onset potential, whereas the GQDs-Fe afforded overpotential of 223 mV (defined as the potential at 50 mA/cm2), far beyond p-GQDs that only afforded 442 mV. Apparently, this outcome unequivocally validated the successful immobilization of molecular Fe2+ ions within the GQDs matrix, consequently leading to the activation of the molecular Fe sites. Addressing the origin of the electrocatalytic OER activity, the redox potential and electrochemical profiles of p-GQDs and GQDs-Fe were further investigated through CV curves. Correspondingly, the CV profile of GQDs-Fe (Fig. 4 b) exhibit broader redox wave spanning from 1.3 – 1.4 V (corresponds to the Fe2+/3+), while the p-GQDs’ accounted for only a negligible redox wave. This suggested that the p-GQDs had minor role in the OER activity, while the molecular Fe2+ ions served as critical determinant. In addition, the OER reaction kinetic was comprehensively evaluated by the Tafel slope calculation, which appeared to be another powerful key parameter. As shown in the Fig. 4 c, contrary to the p-GQDs, the GQDs-Fe recorded much lower Tafel slope value of 54.9 mV/dec as compared to that of 93.1 mV/dec of the p-GQDs. This evidence might reveal the more favourable surface condition of the GQDs-Fe to drive the OER pathway, as induced by the immobilization of the molecular Fe2+ ions. The elevated OER electroactivity of electrocatalysts correlated with the disclosure of the active sites was confirmed by calculating the Cdl, which is utilized to estimate the electrocatalysts' relative electrochemical surface area (ECSA). The linear slope fitting is utilized to express the electrode's Cdl, that is directly equivalent to the ECSA. A major increase on the typical ECSA slope was apparently visible, as shown in the Fig. 4 d, valuing from 0.1 mF/cm2 of p-GQDs to 3.94 mF/cm2, indicating the molecular Fe2+ ions played major role in providing active site to host the catalytic reaction. Subsequently, turn-over frequency (TOF) calculation was provided to investigate the intrinsic OER activity of the p-GQDs and GQDs-Fe, as shown on the Figure S5 . Notably, at an overpotential of 0.33 V, GQDs-Fe demonstrated a calculated TOF value of 0.22 s−1, a remarkable tenfold augmentation compared to the comparatively modest TOF value of 0.032 s−1 exhibited by p-GQDs, that strongly implies a substantial boost in OER activity following the immobilization of Fe ions. The discussion about the origin of the catalytic site will be discussed further in the following chapter.

For a comparison, we conducted an analogous strategy to immobilize the molecular ions according to the previous study, in which using facile hydrothermal method [18]. The Fe(acac)2 as the precursor was diluted into DMF solution followed by GQDs then heated under moderate condition (80 °C), denoted as GQDs-Fex. Upon the mild condition, the as-synthesized material exhibited oxide structure, as revealed by the XRD ( Figure S6
a), where the apparent diffraction peaks are well match indexed to Fe3O4 (JCPDS no. 65–3107). Hence, the mild state was insufficient to immobilize the molecular Fe2+ ions and triggered ion transformation. We further investigated the OER performance, whereas the linear polarization curves (shown in the Figure S6
c) displayed inferior OER activity of GQDs-Fex (318 mV) to that of GQDs-Fe (223 mV) at current density of 50 mA/cm2. Once again, this reaffirmed the distinct catalytic activity of the molecular catalyst owing to their near-atomic scale that achieved the maximum catalytic sites to host catalytic activity.

To further broaden the possibility of the molecular ions, we conducted synthesis process for several different ions, i.e Ni and Co that derived from both of the similar acetylacetonate (−acac2) complex, abbreviated as GQDs-Ni and GQDs-Co, as shown in the Figure S7
a-b. The catalytic performances were recorded in Figure S8 , whereas the collected polarization curve ( Figure S8
a) showed that both of the GQDs-Ni and GQDs-Co exhibited major OER activity improvement as compared to that of the p-GQDs only. Thus, those results confirmed that the heterogenization of the molecular ions (Ni2+ and Co2+) serving as the dominant sites of the catalytic reaction, in which OER. However, those performance were inferior to that of the GQDs-Fe, that most likely contributed by the less immobilization of the molecular ions on the matrix of GQDs. The EDX analysis ( Table S2 , ESI) indicated a relatively less Fe content than that of Ni and Co, that might challenge the immobilization process. In well agreement with the LSV curves, the corresponding Tafel slope ( Figure S8
b) showed far lower value of GQDs-Ni (85.9 mV/dec) and GQDs-Co (62.0 mV/dec) as compared to that of p-GQDs (93.1 mV/dec), which confirmed better OER reaction kinetic and more favourable condition in driving the reaction. In terms of OER's catalytic properties, the EIS spectra, illustrated in Figure S8
c via a Nyquist plot, showed a distinctive depressed semicircle, confirming a charge-transfer process. The charge transfer resistance (R ct) decreased with the immobilization of molecular ions. Notably, GQDs-Fe exhibited the smallest R ct value, surpassing GQDs-Co and GQDs-Ni, aligned well with the LSV. This R ct hierarchy corresponds to a rapid kinetics profile for OER upon the molecular ion immobilization. Similarly, the linear slope fitting of the ECSA ( Figure S8
d) showed significant increase on the slope value of GQDs-Ni and GQDs-Co, affording 1.74 mF/cm2 and 2.92 mF/cm2, respectively, confirmed the increasing of exposed active sites following immobilization process. It is worth noting, however, that these values fall short in comparison to GQDs-Fe, that might confirm the persistence challenge on the immobilization process of GQDs-Ni and GQDs-Co. Those above result had provided fruitful insight on the key role of the molecular ions to the OER performances, that bridging the gap between the molecular catalyst immobilization to the OER field.

Investigating the electrocatalytic behaviour of molecular Fe2+ ions immobilized on GQDs, various concentration of the Fe precursor ranging from 0.01 M, 0.1 M and 0.25 M were employed to optimize the OER activity, displayed in the Fig. 5 . The linear polarization curves (Fig. 5 a) implied that among the optimized Fe concentration, 0.01 M demonstrated the maximum OER activity, followed by 0.01 M (and 0.25 M. Concurrently, the corresponding Tafel slope showed identical trends where GQDs-Fe (0.1 M) exhibited lowest Tafel value (54.9 mV/dec), followed by 0.01 M (55.8 mV/dec) and 0.25 M (58.4 mV/dec), as shown in the Fig. 5 b. Interestingly, all the Tafel slope value were remained consistent close to 59 mV/dec, implying that reversible electron transfer happened prior to the rate-determining phase of the OER and had equivalent efficient reaction kinetic rate, which is the major benefit of molecular catalyst [55]. To decipher the structure–activity relationship, XPS analysis was carried out. The full resolution spectra (Fig. 5 c) showed identical elemental profile, C, O, and Fe on both GQDs-Fe (0.25 M) and GQDs-Fe (0.1 M). The corresponding atomic elemental composition were presented in Table S3 (ESI) with no apparent changes were observed. Noticeably, the GQDs-Fe (0.25 M) exhibited higher Fe atomic contents (∼5.1 %) than GQDs-Fe (0.1 M) that only comprises for 1.8 %. In line with the preceding discussion, the inferior OER activity was mostly contributing by the higher Fe2+ ions concentration that un-homogenously distributed over the matrix of GQDs as the sole substrate, resulting in a complex immobilization process.

Tracing back the origin of the OER activity whether it stems from the matrices of graphene quantum dots or the presence of Fe2+ ions, we conducted a controlled experiment. Targeting the displacement of Fe2+ ions from the GQD matrix, we employed a controlled experiment known as chelation. This process involved the utilization of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), a potent ligand for metal cations [42]. By chelating the Fe2+ ions, we were able to create an EDTA-Fe complex in the solution as illustrated in Fig. 6
a, which allowed to delve deeper investigation. To begin with, the GQDs-Fe that had been drop casted onto the Ni foam was immersed into the EDTA/KOH solution (5 mM) for 12 h under the ambient temperature. The LSV polarization curve (Fig. 6 b) showed significant drop on the OER activity after chelation process, in a similar way as the CV curves looked featureless on the redox after (Fig. 6 c). The chelation phenomenon could be probed by UV–Vis spectra, where it showed absorbance characteristic of d-d transition of EDTA-Fe complex [56] in the solution at 650 nm (Fig. 6 d), suggesting the viable detachment of the Fe2+ ions as the metal sites from GQDs. Heretofore, the GQDs’ contribution to OER activity was trivial, and Fe2+ ions was solely crucial. Thereby, the OER behaviour was originating from the Fe2+ ions rather than GQDs. As the matter of fact, these Fe2+ ions were catalytically active upon immobilization, and served as molecular Fe sites within the GQDs. To the best of our knowledge, this result further implied that the Fe2+ ions were remained in a molecular level, consistent with the previous result. Most importantly, we have achieved significant breakthrough by a successful immobilization of the Fe2+ ions onto GQDs that function as molecular Fe sites, marking a pioneering step for OER revolution. To a same extent, we performed a series of chelation experiment to the GQDs-Ni and GQDs-Co, the results were demonstrated in Figure S9-S10 . Surprisingly, the similar behaviour were observed for both GQDs-Ni and GQDs-Co, where significant drop were noticed in LSV polarization curves ( Figure S9
a and S10a), as well as the loss of the redox features on the CV curves ( Figure S9
b and S10b). The UV–Vis spectra displayed absorbance characteristic of EDTA-Ni complex d-d transitions at 590 nm ( Figure S9
c), whilst the EDTA-Co complex d-d transition were spotted at 480 nm ( Figure S10
c). These outcomes verified the above-mentioned result that the both of the Ni2+ ions and Co2+ ions played essential role in the OER activity, and those OER activity was solely originating from the metal sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of the OH− ions at the molecular Fe sites
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrolyte-to-electrode mass diffusion played as critical step in the OER stage which determine the reaction rates. Therefore, we investigated the OH− ions adsorption (reactants of OER) at the molecular Fe sites. Notwithstanding the fact that the Fe sites were successfully immobilized on GQDs, the constant redox currents plot of each metal sites (Fe, Ni, and Co) exhibited a linear correlation with the square root of the potential scan speeds in CVs scan spanning from 5 to 100 mV/s ( Figure S11
b-14b). This finding strongly indicated that the rate-limiting stage in the redox process is driven by mass transfer from the electrolyte to the electrode, highlighting its significance. The redox reactions of metal sites, especially Fe sites, in alkaline solutions are typically associated by the simultaneous transfer of hydroxide (OH−) ions, as illustrated in the Fig. 7
a [57]. The reaction rates was determined by calculating the redox constants (ks ) derived from the Laviron equation [43]. The calculated redox constants were summarized In the Fig. 7 b, and it is showed that the GQDs-Fe exhibit the highest ks , valuing over than 0.52/s, followed by GQDs-Co (0.43/s) and GQDs-Ni (0.40/s). This results suggested that the adsorption of OH− ions and their coupling with the Fe sites occur at a faster rate compared to the Ni and Co sites. The rapid diffusion rate of OH− ions suggests a preference for the Fe sites in facilitating proton/electron transfer, thereby enhancing the reaction rates. Notably, in a proof-of-principle of the molecular catalyst excellent features, those all redox constant values of the immobilized molecular metal ions on the GQDs are far exceed the redox constant of the p-GQDs that only exhibit minimal value of 0.08/s. This result served as additional confirmation that the metal sites are indeed responsible for the origin of the OER activity, corroborating all the previous results. The kinetic regimes of the OER was highly dependent to the diffusional constraints of the H+/OH− ions, therefore to further evaluating the pH-potential-current relationship, we evaluated the electrocatalytic activity of the GQDs-Fe in an alkaline (KOH) solution with various pH values, ranging from 12 to 14. The acquired redox potentials were plotted against the pH values to construct a Pourbaix diagram, as depicted in Fig. 7 c. Remarkably, the GQDs-Fe’s redox potential displayed linear dependence on the pH values, with slope of 0.062 V/pH. In a theoretical basis, this linear correlation, explained by the Nernst equation, signifies a H+(OH−)/e stoichiometry redox process. The linear slope of 0.059 V/pH involved the H+(OH−)/e stoichiometry of 1, while the slope value of 0.118 V/pH involved to the H+(OH−)/e stoichiometry of 2. Our results indicate a linear slope of 0.062 V/pH, suggesting a H+(OH−)/e stoichiometry of 3/2 for the H+(OH−)/e redox process [58]. Based on our present knowledge, we hypothesize that each Fe site absorbs two OH− ions in a terminal geometry and one OH− ion in a bridging geometry. This arrangement is common in coordination structures based on Fe clusters, which also include oxygen-containing bridging ligands [58–60]. Hence, under these circumstances, we believed that the single Fe sites enabling the exceptional OER catalytic activity of GQDs-Fe.

Elucidating the OER activity, in a proof-of-principle of the application, we compare the catalytic activity of the GQDs-Fe with the commercial OER catalyst, IrO2. The results were depicted in the Figure S15 (ESI). The electrocatalytic activity of GQDs-Fe was significantly higher than that of benchmark electrocatalyst, IrO2, as evidenced by the Figure S15
a. The calculated overpotential value in a different current density were summarized in Figure S15
b, where the GQDs-Fe exhibited ultra-low overpotential in high current density (100 mA/cm2), affording 242 mV, in which 146 mV lower than IrO2 (388 mV). The ultra-low overpotential of the GQDs-Fe in a high current density marked a significant contribution on the OER electrocatalysis field, given the exceptional features of the molecular single Fe sites that successfully activated by immobilizing in GQDs. In addition to the distinctive OER activity of molecular single Fe sites, we performed stability test under the high current density (j = 100 mA/cm2), as shown in the Figure S15
c. Consistent with the rest of the performance evaluations, the GQDs-Fe remains stable under high current density for over 48 h, suggesting their exceptional durability in extreme condition for real applications. In addition, the XPS spectra after stability ( Figure S16 ) revealed the presence of key elemental constituents, such as C, Fe, and O, align well with the XPS spectra before stability ( Fig. S4 ). Notably, the high-scan XPS spectra of Fe 2p exhibited identical deconvolution spectra features even after the stability test, indicating the remarkable durability of the GQDs-Fe. The major deconvolution spectra showed the identical Fe2+ peak at both at characteristic binding energies of 710.0 eV and 723.8 eV, indicating the stable Fe2+ species during the electrolysis process. It is noteworthy that the immobilization of Fe2+ ions activate their catalytic activity, allowing them to function as highly efficient molecular single Fe sites with exceptional catalytic performance.

In summary, we have successfully immobilized Fe2+ ions into the GQDs to form a heterogenous catalyst and activated their catalytic activity to serve as molecularly well-defined single Fe sites for OER in alkaline medium. We demonstrated that the catalytic activity of the molecular catalysts could be activated through the immobilization of the metal ions into graphene’s matrices that substantially increase their OER performance. Upon immobilization, the molecular single Fe sites exhibited remarkable OER performance, achieving ultra-low overpotential of 223 mV (at current density of 50 mA/cm2), outperform the benchmark catalyst, IrO2 that only achieved 357 mV. The structural investigations revealed that the molecular Fe sites were bridged to GQDs through their O-functional group, occupying the defective sites of the GQDs. Moreover, those molecular Fe sites served as the sole center of the OER-active sites with excellent catalytic activity. Our distinctive result showed that the Fe sites remained dispersed in a molecular level on the GQDs matrices, that marked off the OER performance with some oxide-based catalyst. Besides, we could pinpoint the OH− adsorption configurations within the molecular Fe sites, highlighting their tunability. Of the sum, these results marking a pioneering step of a novel, molecularly level single Fe sites that immobilized in a quantum level graphene matrices as a heterogeneous catalyst for OER that shed a light into the OER field.
